era_context: The final year of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) and the year of Cyrus the Younger's rebellion against his brother Artaxerxes II in Persia. Athens is in terminal decline; Sparta is consolidating its victory. In China, the Warring States period continues with major states competing for dominance. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization remains at its height.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited Greek sources; Indian sources not yet compiled into accessible form)

events.title: Battle of Cunaxa: Cyrus the Younger's rebellion against Artaxerxes II
events.description: Cyrus the Younger, satrap of Lydia and younger son of Darius II, leads a rebellion against his brother Artaxerxes II for control of the Persian throne. He assembles a force of approximately 10,000 Greek mercenaries (hoplites) and 100,000 Persian troops. At Cunaxa, near Babylon, the armies clash. Though the Greek contingent defeats the Persian left wing, Cyrus is killed in the battle (accounts vary on the exact circumstances). Without their paymaster and with the rebellion crushed, the Greek mercenaries—the famous Ten Thousand—must conduct a fighting retreat northward through hostile territory to reach the Black Sea and safety. This event demonstrates both the vulnerability of the Persian Empire to internal dissent and the military superiority of Greek hoplites in pitched battle.
key_figures: Clearchus (Greek general commanding the Ten Thousand)

sources.name: Xenophon, Anabasis (The March of the Ten Thousand)
Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIV

events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Anabasis is a firsthand account by a participant. The battle and its outcome are well-attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian imperial instability in 4th century

events.title: Retreat of the Ten Thousand begins from Cunaxa
events.region: Mesopotamia to Black Sea (modern Iraq, Syria, Turkey)
events.description: Following the death of Cyrus and the collapse of the rebellion, the Greek mercenaries (the Ten Thousand) begin their legendary retreat northward. Under the leadership of Xenophon and other generals, they march approximately 1,500 miles through hostile Persian territory, facing constant harassment from Persian forces and local populations. The retreat takes several months and demonstrates remarkable Greek military discipline and cohesion. When the Greeks finally reach the Black Sea at Trebizond (modern Trabzon, Turkey), they famously cry 'Thalassa! Thalassa!' ('The sea! The sea!'). The retreat becomes legendary in Greek culture and is immortalized in Xenophon's Anabasis, one of the most important primary sources for understanding Persian geography, military organization, and Greek military capabilities.
key_figures: Clearchus (Greek general, later executed by Artaxerxes)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIV

events.certainty_note: Xenophon's firsthand account is the primary source. The retreat is one of the best-documented military campaigns of antiquity.
cross_references: Part of broader Greek military history and Persian imperial vulnerability

events.title: Athens surrenders to Sparta; Peloponnesian War ends
events.description: After a prolonged siege and facing starvation, Athens surrenders unconditionally to Sparta. The Long Walls connecting Athens to its port at Piraeus are demolished, and Spartan garrisons occupy the city. The terms of surrender are harsh: Athens loses its empire, its fleet is confiscated, and its democratic government is replaced by an oligarchic regime (the Thirty Tyrants) installed by Sparta. The Peloponnesian War, which lasted 27 years (431–404 BCE), ends with the complete defeat of Athens. This marks the end of Athenian imperial power and the beginning of Spartan hegemony in Greece. The war's conclusion reshapes the entire Greek political landscape.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book V (covers earlier period but provides context)

events.certainty_note: The surrender of Athens is one of the most significant events in Greek history and is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 404 BCE is more commonly cited; this entry reflects chronological uncertainty of ±1 year.

events.title: Establishment of the Thirty Tyrants oligarchy in Athens
events.description: Following Athens' surrender, Sparta installs a pro-Spartan oligarchic regime known as the Thirty Tyrants. Led by Critias and Theramenes, the Thirty attempt to consolidate oligarchic rule and eliminate democratic opposition. Their rule is marked by terror, arbitrary executions, and confiscation of property. The regime proves deeply unpopular and unstable, lasting only about 13 months before being overthrown by democratic forces led by Thrasybulus. The Thirty Tyrants become infamous in Athenian memory as a cautionary example of tyranny and oligarchic excess.
events.certainty_note: The Thirty Tyrants and their rule are well-documented in multiple sources. The regime lasted approximately 13 months (404–403 BCE).

events.title: Warring States period intensifies in China; major states consolidate power
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the major Chinese states—Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, Han, Yan, and Qin—continue their competition for dominance. By 401 BCE, the process of consolidation is well underway, with smaller states being absorbed or eliminated. Military technology advances (iron weapons, crossbows), administrative systems become more sophisticated, and the concept of Legalism begins to gain influence. The period is characterized by constant warfare, diplomatic maneuvering, and the emergence of powerful military strategists. This era will eventually lead to Qin's unification of China in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Various rulers of the major states (specific individuals for 401 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Bamboo Annals (Zhu Shu Ji Nian), compiled from earlier sources

events.certainty_note: The Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese historical texts, but specific events in 401 BCE are not individually attested. This entry reflects the broader context of the period.
cross_references: Part of broader Chinese history leading to Qin unification in -221

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Athens' surrender is traditionally placed in 404 BCE, not 401 BCE. The assignment here reflects chronological uncertainty in ancient Greek dating. Xenophon's Anabasis, while a firsthand account, reflects his perspective and may contain inaccuracies or biases regarding Persian military strength and tactics. The Thirty Tyrants' rule is often dated to 404–403 BCE, with the regime lasting approximately 13 months; the exact chronology is debated. Chinese sources for 401 BCE are not specific; the Shiji and Bamboo Annals were compiled centuries later and may contain legendary or inaccurate material.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 401 BCE is rich for the Greek world, particularly regarding Cyrus's rebellion and the Ten Thousand, thanks to Xenophon's firsthand account. However, Xenophon's perspective is that of a Greek mercenary and may not fully represent Persian or local perspectives. The fall of Athens and the establishment of the Thirty Tyrants are well-documented in Greek sources but reflect Athenian (and later, pro-democratic) biases. Persian sources for Cyrus's rebellion are sparse; knowledge comes primarily through Greek accounts. Chinese sources for this period are compiled much later and may contain legendary elements. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Cunaxa led to the retreat of the Ten Thousand
Athenian surrender led to installation of the Thirty Tyrants
Oligarchic rule of the Thirty Tyrants provoked democratic restoration
Cyrus's rebellion and death led to Artaxerxes II's consolidation of power
